Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal clause with possessed noun phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone becomes emotionally overwhelmed or loses emotional balance
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ja’ ya xk’uxub yo’tan sok ya xjel sba
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in highland Tzeltal communities to describe deep sadness linked to the heart as seat of emotion
First Example Sentence: Te me’ile ya xch’ay ta sba yo’tan te ayix ta laj stz’ib te carta.
Second Example Sentence: Ya xch’ay ta sba yo’tan jich ma’yuk mach’a ya yich’ sk’oplal.
Third Example Sentence: Ta pat o’tanil ya xch’ay ta sba yo’tan te kereme.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks arrogantly or boasts excessively
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ya xnop sba lek sok ya xch’un mi más sna’
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to traditional oral culture where humility in speech is socially valued
First Example Sentence: Muk’ul sba ta jk’op te winike ta tsobajel.
Second Example Sentence: Ay mach’a ya xk’uxet, pero muk’ul sba ta jk’op jichuk.
Third Example Sentence: Te ajwalile muk’ul sba ta jk’op sok ma’yuk spas.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person talks constantly without action
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ya xk’opon ep pero ma’yuk spasel
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from communal critique of empty promises in village assemblies
First Example Sentence: Te alale ya xwe’ ta sti’ ta ora te ya yalbe sk’oplal.
Second Example Sentence: Ma meleluk ya spas, ya xwe’ ta sti’ jich no’ox.
Third Example Sentence: Te kereme ya xwe’ ta sti’ pero ma baht ta a’tel.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Heart metaphors are central in Tzeltal moral discourse
First Example Sentence: K’uxbil yo’tan te antse, laj yak’ swe’el te me’iletike.
Second Example Sentence: Ta sna te mamale ay jun k’uxbil yo’tan xchi’uk smalal.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Dynamic verb phrase with body-part metaphor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Eye-related metaphors frequently indicate deception and perception
First Example Sentence: Te ajchonole ya xpet ta sat te jlumaltike.
Second Example Sentence: Ma xch’un te winike, ya xpet ta sat jich.
Third Example Sentence: Ay mach’a ya xpet ta sat ta pasel contrato.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person filled with anger or jealousy
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ya xk’uxet sok ya xilian yo’tan
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Color symbolism links redness with emotional heat in Maya traditions
First Example Sentence: Tsajal yo’tan te antse ta sk’eojibal.
Second Example Sentence: Te kereme tsajal yo’tan ta yilbel te yajnil.
Third Example Sentence: Ya xcha’be sk’oplal te tsajal yo’tan bankile.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone has lost courage or confidence
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ya xbibaj sba sok ma xch’un sba
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Often heard in testimonies about displacement and poverty
First Example Sentence: Ma’yuk xa stukel yo’tan te tatike ta nopjun.
Second Example Sentence: Te antse ma’yuk xa stukel yo’tan ta stojol a’tel.
Third Example Sentence: Banti ay wocol, ma’yuk xa stukel yo’tan te winike.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A feeling or idea gradually grows stronger
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Growth metaphors tied to maize cultivation and life cycles
First Example Sentence: Ya xch’ij ta yo’tan te sk’uxlejale.
Second Example Sentence: Ta slamal k’inal ya xch’ij ta yo’tan te smelol.
Third Example Sentence: Ya xch’ij ta yo’tan te slekilal sok te bats’i k’op.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A clever or tricky person who notices everything
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The number nine symbolizes multiplicity and heightened perception in Maya cosmology
First Example Sentence: Bolon satil te bankile, ma’yuk mach’a ya xlo’la yu’un.
Second Example Sentence: Ta tsobajel laj yalik mi bolon satil te ajk’opojel.
Third Example Sentence: Te xut ya xch’un mi bolon satil jich te yajnil.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verbal idiom with possessed directional noun
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone loses direction morally or socially
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Road metaphors are deeply embedded in Maya ethical teachings
First Example Sentence: Te kereme ya xch’ay sbej ta lum k’inal.
Second Example Sentence: Mi ma xich’ mantal, ya xch’ay sbej te alale.
Third Example Sentence: Ay epetik te ya xch’ay sbej ta stojol tak’in.
